description: A potential customer arrives, observes the queue, and decides not to join because the queue is too long or wait time appears too high.
A customer who has already joined the queue leaves before being served, typically because their wait exceeds a patience threshold.
A customer switches between multiple queues during their wait, typically joining a shorter queue when one becomes available.
The model includes multiple classes of entities (e.g., regular vs. PreCheck passengers, new vs. follow-up patients) with different service times, priorities, or routing behaviors.
Resources have time-varying availability driven by shift schedules, breaks, or calendar-based patterns rather than being available for the full simulation horizon.
Resources can fail during operation and require repair, typically modeled with mean-time-between-failures and mean-time-to-repair distributions.
Resources require setup/changeover time between different job types (e.g., paint color changes, mold changes, tool changes in manufacturing).
Resources have varying capabilities — some can perform more activity types or at different speeds than others (e.g., senior vs. junior staff, specialized vs. generalist equipment).
A resource is shared across multiple activities or multiple subsystems, creating contention between otherwise-independent flows.
The arrival rate varies over the simulation horizon, typically modeled as a non-homogeneous Poisson process or via piecewise-constant rates across intervals (e.g., 15-minute buckets across a day).
Multiple entities arrive simultaneously as a batch rather than one at a time (e.g., a bus of passengers arriving at a TSA checkpoint).
Arriving entities carry priority attributes that influence their downstream queueing and routing decisions.
Arrivals are bound to scheduled appointment slots with stochastic punctuality (early, on-time, late, no-show) rather than purely random inter-arrival times.

Arrivals are driven by a deterministic takt time or near-fixed cadence rather than a stochastic arrival process. Characteristic of manufacturing assembly lines where the line runs at a target production rate and variability enters through service times rather than arrivals.
Routing decisions depend on dynamic system state (e.g., congestion level, queue length, time of day) rather than fixed probabilities or static rules.
Routing outcomes are determined by Bernoulli or multinomial draws (e.g., a fraction of entities are routed to secondary inspection, the rest proceed).
Entities observe multiple parallel queues and join the one with the fewest waiting entities (also called join-the-shortest-queue or JSQ).
Entities can revisit earlier activities via rework, retry, or rescan loops (e.g., a slide flagged for QC failure is returned for re-preparation).
Multiple paths through the system execute in parallel with later synchronization or merge points (e.g., dual-lane drive-thru merging at pickup).
Queues have a finite physical capacity limit (e.g., drive-thru lane length, waiting room chairs) beyond which additional arrivals cannot enter.
Queue service order is determined by entity priority rather than first-in-first-out (FIFO). High-priority entities can jump ahead of lower-priority ones.
Last-in-first-out service order — common in stack-like systems (e.g., document archival, push-down stores).
Shortest-job-first service discipline where the entity with the smallest expected service time is selected next from the queue.
The model tracks multiple entity classes as first-class concepts, with class attributes affecting behavior throughout the system.
The simulation has an explicit initial transient period whose statistics are discarded before collecting steady-state metrics.
The model uses techniques like importance sampling or rare-event simulation to estimate probabilities of low-probability outcomes (e.g., system overload, equipment failure).
Model behavior varies explicitly by time of day — shift changes, peak periods, breakfast/lunch/dinner windows, overnight operations — beyond just arrival rate variation.
